title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Storms expose Warren's aging grid as towns across Michigan hit pause on data centers.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're digging into a power outage that has Warren officials pushing for an immediate moratorium on new data centers.
That sounds like the latest flashpoint in a statewide wave of pushback.
Exactly. Council Secretary Mindy Moore called for a July 14 vote after storms knocked down dozens of power lines in Warren.
So the city's aging grid couldn't handle the storm, and now they're linking that directly to data center concerns?
Yes. If the moratorium passes, Warren would join a growing list of Michigan communities hitting the brakes while they draft zoning rules.
How many towns are we talking about now? It feels like this has been building for weeks.
Today alone Jackson approved a full halt on new proposals to study power and water impacts first.
And Lansing did the same yesterday with a six-month freeze.
Right. Haring Township saw hundreds turn out demanding an outright ban, and Lowell Township has its first vote tonight.
What's really at stake for residents in places like Warren? Higher bills? Noise? Traffic?
All of the above. Neighbors worry about round-the-clock power draw straining an already fragile grid, plus noise, heavy truck traffic, and potential spikes in utility costs.
I keep hearing about farmland and water too. How does that fit into the Warren situation?
Warren is more suburban, but the same concerns echo across the state. Data centers need massive water for cooling and can consume land that might otherwise stay agricultural or residential.
Has Warren had data center proposals already, or is this preemptive?
The push is preemptive in the sense that they're writing rules before projects lock in sites, but the storm damage made the timing urgent.
What happens if the moratorium passes tonight?
It would pause new applications while the city studies impacts and crafts zoning. Similar to what Jackson and Lansing are doing.
And if it fails?
Projects could move forward under current rules, which many residents say don't adequately protect against noise, traffic, or rate hikes.
This feels like a pattern repeating across Oakland, Macomb, Washtenaw, and beyond.
It is. Wixom residents are getting their say on new rules at tonight's city council meeting too.
What are they most worried about there?
Noise, traffic, and strain on local power near potential sites in that Oakland County suburb.
Benton Harbor had a meeting today where a hyperscale developer faced residents over those exact issues.
Yes. Neighbors questioned whether local roads and the electric system can handle constant heavy traffic and nonstop power demand.
Monroe voters rejected a plan outright over energy and noise fears.
They did. The Monroe Times reported opposition prevailed, keeping zoning rules unchanged for now.
Salem Township in Washtenaw County is hearing a revised plan that promises lower noise and less visible equipment.
Neighbors there remain skeptical the improvements will hold once construction starts.
Meanwhile, there's political money in the mix too.
Michigan official Benson is under fire for accepting donations from Big Tech companies racing to build these facilities.
Critics say that undercuts efforts to protect residents from higher costs and land conflicts.
It's highlighting how quickly these projects are turning into a political issue statewide.
So pulling back, what's the bigger picture for electric bills if multiple data centers land in one area?
Residents in Jackson and elsewhere fear that concentrated demand could drive up rates without delivering meaningful local tax relief.
And water use? These facilities need a lot for cooling, right?
They do. That's why Jackson specifically cited water impacts in their pause decision.
Farmland conversion is another flashpoint we've seen in rural townships.
Haring Township residents explicitly want to keep their area off-limits to preserve rural character.
What happens next in Warren if the vote goes through?
Officials would have time to study the grid, write rules, and decide where — if anywhere — data centers fit.
Similar to the six-month freeze Lansing approved yesterday.
Exactly. It buys breathing room for communities feeling overwhelmed by the pace of proposals.
Do we know how many gigawatts these projects typically demand?
Individual hyperscale sites can require hundreds of megawatts, sometimes approaching a gigawatt, which is why utilities and towns are scrambling.
That puts real pressure on aging infrastructure like Warren's.
It does. The storm just made the vulnerability impossible to ignore.
Any sense of how long these moratoriums usually last?
Varies. Lansing went six months. Jackson didn't set an end date but wants rules in place first.
And for Warren specifically, the request is for a moratorium vote tonight.
Yes. We'll know soon whether Macomb County's second-largest city joins the pause trend.
Moving to quick hits, let's start with Wixom's meeting tonight.
Residents near potential sites will voice concerns about noise, traffic, and power strain as the council hears input on a proposed ordinance.
That vote could set the template for future projects in that suburb.
Jackson's pause today gives the city time to study cumulative impacts before any sites are locked in.
Benton Harbor residents grilled a developer on noise and traffic at their council session.
Monroe voters blocked a project outright after energy and noise worries won out.
Salem Township is reviewing a revised design meant to address visibility and sound concerns.
And political donations to state official Benson from data center companies are drawing scrutiny.
Yesterday's stories include Lansing's six-month freeze and strong turnout in Haring Township for a potential ban.
Lowell Township in Kent County also faces its first data center vote tonight.
Now for on deck — what meetings can listeners actually attend?
Tonight in Saline Township the board will vote on a 12-year tax abatement request tied to an Oracle-OpenAI hyperscale project.
There's also a special meeting there considering a 50 percent property tax reduction for 12 years on that same project.
Tomorrow in Ypsilanti Township there's an informational session on the University of Michigan/Los Alamos data center project updates.
That includes history, legislative developments, opposition, and resource concerns, with time for Q&A.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Warren Grid Failures Spark Fresh Data Center Moratorium Push
